questions.question: What is an isogeny between elliptic curves?

options.text: A group homomorphism that is also a morphism of varieties

questions.explanation: An isogeny is a morphism (algebraic map) between elliptic curves that preserves the group structure: φ(P + Q) = φ(P) + φ(Q). It's both geometric and algebraic.

questions.question: What is the degree of an isogeny φ?

options.text: The number of points on the curve
The j-invariant of the target curve

questions.explanation: The degree of an isogeny equals the size of its kernel. For a degree-ℓ isogeny, exactly ℓ points map to the identity O.

questions.question: What is the relationship between φ̂ ∘ φ and the degree of φ?
questions.explanation: The composition φ̂ ∘ φ equals the multiplication-by-degree map [deg(φ)]. This is a fundamental property connecting dual isogenies.

questions.question: What do Vélu's formulas compute?

options.text: The number of points on a curve
The isogeny and codomain curve from a kernel subgroup

questions.explanation: Vélu's formulas take a curve E and a finite subgroup G, and compute both the isogeny φ: E → E/G and the coefficients of the codomain curve E/G.

questions.question: In the supersingular ℓ-isogeny graph, how many edges leave each vertex?
questions.explanation: The supersingular ℓ-isogeny graph is (ℓ + 1)-regular: each vertex (j-invariant) has exactly ℓ + 1 outgoing edges (ℓ-isogenies).

questions.question: Why are supersingular isogeny graphs called Ramanujan graphs?

options.text: They have optimal expansion properties (eigenvalue bounds)
They have prime number of vertices
They are named after a Ramanujan conjecture

questions.explanation: Ramanujan graphs have optimal spectral expansion: their non-trivial eigenvalues are bounded. This makes random walks mix rapidly, which was believed to provide security.

questions.question: How is composition of isogenies related to their degrees?
questions.explanation: Degrees are multiplicative: deg(φ₂ ∘ φ₁) = deg(φ₁) × deg(φ₂). This is why composing a small 2-isogenies gives a 2ᵃ-isogeny.

questions.question: Every finite subgroup G of an elliptic curve E uniquely determines an isogeny with kernel G.
questions.explanation: True! This is a fundamental theorem: there's a bijection between finite subgroups G ⊂ E and isogenies φ: E → E/G (up to isomorphism of the codomain).
